title: InFoBench: Evaluating Instruction Following Ability in Large Language Models
venue: arXiv preprint v1 (submitted 7 Jan 2024); listed as Findings of ACL 2024 per the citation the task provided. Note per the extraction header: the ACL Findings 2024 venue label was not independently confirmed from the arXiv abs page's own journal-ref/comment field in the fetch used, though it is consistent with public record.
claim: The paper introduces the Decomposed Requirements Following Ratio (DRFR), a metric that decomposes each complex instruction into a set of simpler, atomic YES/NO criteria so that an LLM's compliance can be judged per-requirement rather than with a single holistic score. Paired with DRFR, the paper releases InFoBench, a 500-instruction benchmark (an Easy Set and a harder, more diverse, manually curated Hard Set) with 2,250 decomposed questions, and shows via human-annotator agreement experiments that DRFR is more reliable than traditional Direct Scoring, and that GPT-4 with a structured multi-turn prompt is a cost-effective, accurate substitute for human annotators for this decomposed scoring.
novelty: The authors present DRFR as a new, more interpretable and more reliable alternative to (a) pairwise/A/B testing and Elo-style comparisons, which require O(N^2) comparisons and are hard to interpret, and (b) Direct Scoring (1-5 holistic ratings), which the paper shows has lower inter-annotator agreement than the decomposed approach. The second claimed novelty is the Hard Set itself: a manually curated, 72-domain, higher-constraint-density instruction set built specifically to address three weaknesses of the prior Easy Set (from Wang et al. 2022 / Self-Instruct lineage) -- namely (1) simple instructions that fail to differentiate modern LLMs, (2) low constraint-type diversity (~3 constraints/instruction), and (3) subjective/ambiguous requirements (e.g., 'helpful'). A third claimed contribution is the empirical finding that GPT-4, used as an annotator with a structured multi-turn (sequential decomposed-question) prompt, is both accurate and far cheaper/faster than expert or AMT crowd annotation, making automatic DRFR scoring practical at scale (Sec. 3.2, Table 4). The Related Work section explicitly positions InFoBench against two concurrent efforts: FollowBench (Jiang et al. 2023), which sequentially stacks fine-grained constraints into multi-level instructions, and IFEval (Zhou et al. 2023), which emphasizes objective, verifiable instructions over a smaller (25) instruction set, contrasting with InFoBench's 'broader coverage yet similar focus on objectivity' (Sec. 5).

protocol.description: InFoBench is fundamentally an evaluation-side (judge) protocol layered on top of single-shot generation. Generation: each of the six evaluated LLMs receives a single instruction (the 'Total Instruction' = 'Actual Instruction' + optional 'Input') and produces one generated text via a single forward pass with greedy decoding (temperature=0, top_p=1; Sec. 4, footnote 9) -- this generation step is single-shot with no interaction loop. Scoring/annotation: the paper's central methodological contribution is a multi-turn dialogue with the GPT-4 judge. 'As for multiple decomposed questions, We engage in a multi-turn dialogue with GPT-4. Each instruction and generation is followed by several questions, asked sequentially. GPT-4's response to each question is used as context for the subsequent question.' (Appendix A.2). The paper explicitly compared this sequential multi-turn approach against asking all decomposed questions simultaneously and asking each question fully independently, and 'found the sequential multi-turn dialogue to yield more effective results' (Appendix A.2) -- so the interactive, context-carrying judge protocol was chosen over both a single-shot all-at-once variant and a fully independent per-question variant.
protocol.observation_content: Per the judge/annotator's per-turn view (Appendix A.2, Table 7 example): the GPT-4 judge is given a system-style instruction block describing the YES/NO rubric ('YES: Select YES if the generated text entirely fulfills the condition... even minor inaccuracies exclude the text from receiving a YES rating... NO: Opt for NO if the generated text fails to meet the question's requirements or provides no information...'), followed by the 'Input' (if any) and the 'Generated Text' once, and then a sequence of individual decomposed Questions asked one at a time in successive <User>/<Assistant> turns, with each prior YES/NO answer remaining in context for subsequent questions. Notably, 'the Actual Instruction is not included in the Decomposed Protocol's prompt, as we hypothesize that the decomposed questions encompass the instruction's requirements' (Appendix A.2) -- i.e. the judge sees only Input + Generated Text + one question per turn, not the original instruction text itself. Few-shot examples were tried in this prompt and found not to help: 'We also experimented with incorporating a few-shot learning approach by providing examples in the prompt. However, the results indicated no significant improvement, possibly due to the varied nature of the instructions and questions, and the simplicity of the answer format.' (Appendix A.2). For the model-under-test (generation) side, observation content is simply the Total Instruction (instruction + optional input) in one shot; no demonstrations or feedback loop are used for generation itself.

variants_compared: Direct Scoring (DS): holistic 1-5 human rating of the whole response vs. Decomposed (DRFR): binary YES/NO per atomic requirement, aggregated as a ratio (Sec. 3.1).
Annotation source: human expert vs. AMT crowd-worker vs. GPT-4 (gpt-4-0314) as the DRFR annotator (Sec. 3.2, Table 4).

Judge prompting strategy for multiple decomposed questions: (a) sequential multi-turn dialogue (chosen), (b) asking all questions simultaneously, (c) asking each question independently -- 'found the sequential multi-turn dialogue to yield more effective results' (Appendix A.2); no numeric ablation table given for (b)/(c), described only qualitatively.
Few-shot vs zero-shot judge prompt: tried adding examples to the decomposed-question prompt; 'no significant improvement' (Appendix A.2) -- no numbers reported, described qualitatively only.

Judge model version: gpt-4-0314 (main results, Table 5) vs gpt-4-1106-preview (Appendix A.4, Table 8) -- 'the scores attributed by gpt-4-1106-preview are marginally lower than those given by its predecessor, gpt-4-0314' (Appendix A.4).
representations.effect_on_accuracy: DRFR vs DS: Pairwise Kappa Agreement is much higher for DRFR (Overall 0.532) than DS (Overall 0.284) across 50 instructions (Table 3) -- 'a moderate agreement' vs 'a fair agreement' band per the paper's own Fleiss-Kappa interpretation scale. Annotation source: Expert ACC is defined as 100% (it is the gold standard), GPT-4 reaches 89% overall accuracy vs AMT's 84% overall accuracy (Table 4), while GPT-4 costs $19.40 vs Expert's $1,416.00 and AMT's $187.50 for the same 50-instruction slice, and takes 60 minutes vs Expert's 1,440 minutes and AMT's 2,160 minutes (Table 4).

models.name: GPT-4 (gpt-4-1106-preview)
models.key_results: Table 5: Easy 90.1, Hard 89.1, Overall 89.4 (annotated by gpt-4-0314). Table 8 (annotated by gpt-4-1106-preview instead): Easy 85.7, Hard 85.4, Overall 85.5. Highest-scoring model overall in both annotation passes; paper notes 'even with this state-of-the-art model, more than 10% of the requirements remain unfulfilled' (Sec. 4).

models.name: GPT-3.5-turbo-1106
models.key_results: Table 5: Easy 90.4, Hard 85.1, Overall 86.7. Table 8: Easy 82.5, Hard 81.2, Overall 81.6.

models.name: Claude-2.1
models.key_results: Table 5: Easy 82.9, Hard 87.9, Overall 86.4 -- the only model of the six that scores higher on Hard than Easy (Sec. 4 observation 2). Table 8: Easy 77.5, Hard 81.9, Overall 80.5.

models.name: Gemini-Pro
models.key_results: Table 5: Easy 87.3, Hard 84.9, Overall 85.6. Table 8: Easy 79.7, Hard 80.2, Overall 80.1.

models.name: Llama-2-70b-chat
models.key_results: Table 5: Easy 89.6, Hard 82.1, Overall 84.4. Table 8: Easy 84.5, Hard 77.6, Overall 79.7. Open-source model; paper notes closed-source models outperform open-source ones on Hard Set (Sec. 4 observation 3).

models.name: Vicuna-13b-v1.5
models.key_results: Table 5: Easy 86.1, Hard 75.0, Overall 78.4 -- lowest overall score of the six models. Table 8: Easy 79.1, Hard 69.5, Overall 72.4.

models.name: Alpaca-7B, GPT-3.5-turbo, Claude-v1 (used only in the DS vs DRFR reliability study, Sec. 3.1, not in the full six-model automatic evaluation of Sec. 4)
models.key_results: No per-model DRFR/DS scores reported in the extraction beyond their use in generating the 50-instruction pairwise-comparison set for Table 3/Table 4; no table gives individual accuracy numbers for these three separate from GPT-3.5-turbo/GPT-4 already covered above.

headline_numbers: DRFR vs DS Pairwise Kappa Agreement: DS Overall = 0.284, DRFR Overall = 0.532, a gap 'exceeding 0.19' (Table 3, Sec. 3.1).
Annotation source accuracy (Overall, 50 instructions): Expert = 100.0% (gold), GPT-4 = 89.0%, AMT = 84.0% (Table 4, Sec. 3.2).
Annotation cost (Overall, 50 instructions): Expert = $1,416.00 / 1,440 min; AMT = $187.50 / 2,160 min; GPT-4 = $19.40 / 60 min (Table 4).

Six-model automatic DRFR evaluation (gpt-4-0314 annotator), Overall column: gpt-4-1106-preview 89.4, gpt-3.5-turbo-1106 86.7, claude-2.1 86.4, gemini-pro 85.6, Llama-2-70b-chat 84.4, Vicuna-13b-v1.5 78.4 (Table 5, Sec. 4).
Dataset scale: 500 instructions total (Easy 252, Hard 248), 2,250 decomposed questions total (Easy 690, Hard 1,560), #Req./Instruction: Easy 2.74, Hard 6.29, Overall 4.50 (Table 2, Sec. 2.2.3).

Constraint-type performance pattern: 'performance is highest on Content and Style, intermediate on Format, and lowest on Number and Linguistic constraints. This general trend is observed across all six language models' (Sec. 4, Figure 3).
failure_modes: All six models score lowest on Number and Linguistic constraint types (Sec. 4, Figure 3; Appendix A.6 case studies).

Numerical/counting failures: models miscount exact character/word counts (e.g., claiming a string is '20 characters' when it is not) and fail to maintain numeric proportions/ratios in generated content (e.g., a DNA-sequence task requiring exact nucleotide counts and purine/pyrimidine ratios across 6-nucleotide windows) -- 'none of the models fully satisfied the 4th and 5th constraints regarding numbers' (Appendix A.5, A.6, Tables 9-11).

Atypical linguistic-pattern failures: constraints requiring deviation from normal word-prediction patterns (e.g., 'each word begins with the last letter of the previous word' while also covering the full alphabet A-Z) are largely unmet by all models except a partial, coherence-sacrificing attempt by gpt-4-1106-preview; the paper attributes this to LLMs being 'trained to predict subsequent words or characters based on conventional linguistic patterns' (Appendix A.6, Tables 12-13).

Judge (GPT-4) annotation errors identified via disagreement analysis (Appendix A.5): (1) misleading content -- model output that merely repeats the instruction text can fool the judge into a false YES; (2) numerical computation/counting errors by the judge itself, mirroring the generator's own numeric weaknesses; (3) partially-unmet compound requirements where the judge misses that only part of a conjunctive requirement was satisfied; (4) spatial-reasoning requirements (e.g., verifying a claimed 6x6 grid actually has the right dimensions) that the text-only judge cannot properly check; (5) requirements with only partial fulfillment where the judge over-credits a plausible-looking but incomplete answer.

Roughly 30-40% of decomposed questions show medium-to-high human annotator disagreement (Disagreement Level >= 2 out of 0-5), reflecting inherent ambiguity/subjectivity in some decomposed questions even after the authors' curation effort (Appendix A.5, Figure 9).
Even the best model (gpt-4-1106-preview) leaves 'more than 10% of the requirements... unfulfilled' (Sec. 4).
one_shot_vs_multistep: For the model being evaluated, the paper only tests single forward-pass generation (one instruction in, one generated text out, greedy decoding) -- there is no multi-turn or iterative-refinement condition for the generator itself in this paper. The multi-step / interactive design is confined to the judge/scorer: the paper explicitly found that decomposing an instruction into a sequence of atomic questions and posing them to GPT-4 turn-by-turn, carrying forward each prior answer as context, outperformed both a single flattened prompt containing all questions at once and a set of fully independent single-question prompts ('found the sequential multi-turn dialogue to yield more effective results', Appendix A.2). Thus the paper's conclusion is: models can already produce a plausible single-shot answer to a complex, multi-constraint instruction, but accurately *judging* whether that single-shot answer satisfies each of several decomposed sub-requirements benefits from a multi-turn, context-carrying interaction protocol on the evaluator side, not from giving the generator itself an interaction loop.

relation_to_candidate.position: InFoBench sits almost entirely on the evaluation/scoring axis rather than the generation-protocol axis that defines c22. The thing being judged (model output) is produced single-shot from one instruction; what is 'observed' by the object model is the full Total Instruction (possibly plus an Input) in one call, with no feedback loop, no demonstrations that matter (few-shot was tried and discarded), and no per-turn partial feedback to the generator. Output shape is free-form natural-language text of task-dependent length (not a single fact/label, and not a small closed-output-space micro-generation like c22's 'a few words'). The verifier in InFoBench is an LLM-as-judge (GPT-4, prompted with a rubric) answering YES/NO to each decomposed natural-language question, i.e. a soft, model-based verifier operating over subjective-to-moderately-objective criteria -- not a deterministic, unit-tested checker function. The interactivity that does exist in InFoBench (the multi-turn sequential question-answering) lives on the judge side of the pipeline, not the generation side, and it is used to improve scoring reliability, not to give the generator a chance to iterate.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c22: Both works decompose a complex instruction-following task into a set of atomic, independently-checkable sub-requirements and log a per-requirement verdict as a diagnostic signal (InFoBench's r_{i,j} binary YES/NO per decomposed question vs. c22's per-checker pass/fail). Both are explicitly framed as responses to the difficulty of scoring compound, multi-constraint instructions with a single holistic judgment. Both use a constraint-type taxonomy to analyze where models fail (InFoBench: Content/Linguistic/Style/Format/Number; c22: exact word count/forbidden letter/required keyword/casing/end-with token) and both report that Number/format-adjacent constraints are comparatively hard for models.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Verifier type: InFoBench's per-atom check is an LLM judge answering a natural-language YES/NO question (Appendix A.2), which the paper's own error analysis (Appendix A.5) shows is imperfect (numeric-counting errors, spatial-reasoning blind spots, susceptibility to instruction-echoing) -- c22's atoms are deterministic Python checker functions (word count, letter presence, casing, suffix match) with no judge fallibility. (2) Scoring aggregation: InFoBench's DRFR is a continuous ratio (sum of satisfied atoms / total atoms across the dataset, Eq. 1) that explicitly supports partial credit at the dataset level (an instruction can score, e.g., 5/8); c22 scores each individual instance strictly 0/1 (all-or-nothing pass) while only using per-atom verdicts as diagnostics, not as partial credit toward the instance's own score. (3) Instructions in InFoBench are naturalistic, domain-grounded, and manually authored by subject-matter experts (72-143 domains) with no reproducible synthetic generator or seed-based sampling; c22's constraint stack is synthetically/programmatically composed from a fixed checker library plus a COLLIE-style grammar, giving an infinite, seedable, contamination-resistant pool that InFoBench's fixed 500-instruction, human-authored dataset does not have. (4) InFoBench's task/output is a full free-form response to a real-world instruction (essays, DNA sequences, eviction notices, etc.); c22's task is a trivial micro-task ("name a color") with output constrained to a few words -- a much smaller, cheaper generation surface. (5) InFoBench has no analog to c22's conditional Selection constraints ("if the input has property P obey rule A else rule B") that must be discovered rather than stated; InFoBench's decomposed questions are always explicitly derived from and visible in the original instruction (the judge is not given the instruction, but the object model that generates the answer always is).
relation_to_candidate.what_c22_would_add: c22 would add: (a) a fully deterministic, judge-free verifier chain in place of InFoBench's LLM-judge-based per-atom scoring, removing the numeric-counting and spatial-reasoning failure modes the paper documents for its own judge; (b) a synthetic, seedable, infinitely renewable instance generator (vs. InFoBench's fixed, manually-authored, non-scalable 500-instruction set -- explicitly flagged as a Limitation: 'The manual nature of our instruction writing process limits our capacity to scale this dataset significantly', Limitations #2); (c) strict all-or-nothing scoring per instance (sharper pass/fail signal at the instance level) while still retaining per-atom diagnostic logging, an explicit hybrid of InFoBench's partial-credit DRFR philosophy and a stricter scoring floor; (d) the undiscoverable conditional Selection constraint, which has no counterpart in InFoBench's dataset and directly targets the one-shot-enumeration weakness that a fixed, expert-authored, instruction-visible dataset like InFoBench cannot structurally prevent.

corpus_claim: InFoBench / DRFR (Qin et al., Findings of ACL 2024) defines the Decomposed Requirements Following Ratio -- the per-atom partial-credit scoring model c22 logs as diagnostic verdicts (while still scoring 0/1 overall).
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper's Eq. 1 defines DRFR = (sum_{i,j} r_{i,j}) / (sum_i m_i), where each instruction i is decomposed into m_i binary requirements and r_{i,j} in [0,1] denotes whether requirement j of instruction i is met (Sec. 2.1) -- this is precisely a per-atom, partial-credit-style aggregate scoring model (partial credit at the dataset/instruction level, since an instruction's requirements need not all be satisfied for the instruction to contribute a nonzero score to the overall ratio). c22's design, as described in the candidate file, logs analogous per-checker binary verdicts as diagnostic facts while still scoring the instance overall as strict 0/1 (all constraints must pass) -- i.e., c22 borrows InFoBench's decomposed/per-atom verdict logging exactly as the corpus claim states, but intentionally does not adopt DRFR's ratio-based partial credit as its own primary score. One caveat: the venue attribution ('Findings of ACL 2024') is per the extraction header not independently confirmable from the fetched arXiv abs page's own metadata field, though it is consistent with the paper's public record and does not affect the substance of the claim about DRFR itself.
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dials used: InFoBench splits into Easy Set (252 instructions, avg length 17.77 words, avg 2.74 requirements/instruction, from prior Self-Instruct-style data) and Hard Set (248 instructions, avg length 59.26 words, avg 6.29 requirements/instruction, manually authored across 72 domains) (Table 2, Sec. 2.2). Requirement/constraint count per instruction is the paper's own difficulty proxy, directly analogous to c22's 'constraint count and level' dial.

Prompt format that swung judge accuracy: sequential multi-turn per-question dialogue (context carried forward) beat both all-questions-at-once and fully-independent-per-question prompting for the judge (Appendix A.2, qualitative, no numeric ablation given in extraction).
Few-shot examples added to the judge prompt gave 'no significant improvement' (Appendix A.2) -- relevant negative result if designing prompt variants for a c22-style checker-verdict pipeline.

Metrics: DRFR (ratio of satisfied atomic requirements, Eq. 1); Pairwise Kappa Agreement (Fleiss' Kappa on 3-way pairwise model comparisons, Table 3); ACC, PLD0/1/2, and WPLD (Weighted Pairwise Label Distance, Eq. 2) for annotation-source robustness (Table 4).

Floors/ceilings observed: best model (gpt-4-1106-preview) still leaves >10% of requirements unmet overall (Sec. 4); constraint-type floor is Number and Linguistic (lowest across all six models, Figure 3); Vicuna-13b-v1.5 is the overall floor model at 78.4 (gpt-4-0314 annotation, Table 5) / 72.4 (gpt-4-1106-preview annotation, Table 8); human expert annotation is defined as the 100% accuracy ceiling/gold standard by construction (Table 4).

Contamination/data-release status: 'We release our code and dataset at https://github.com/qinyiwei/InfoBench' (Abstract, footnote 1) -- the Easy Set derives from prior public Self-Instruct-style data (Wang et al. 2022) already used across multiple prior evaluations, so it is plausibly contamination-exposed; the Hard Set is the authors' original, manually curated contribution intended to be less contaminated at time of release, but as a small (248-instruction), fixed, publicly released, non-regenerable set it is not immune to future contamination and cannot be scaled synthetically (Limitations #2).

Cost/scale data useful for baseline design: judge (GPT-4-0314) annotation cost for the whole 500-instruction/2250-question benchmark can be extrapolated from the 50-instruction subset figures: $19.40 and 60 minutes for 50 instructions (Table 4) -- i.e., automatic multi-turn LLM-judge annotation is cheap and fast relative to expert ($1,416.00/1,440 min for the same 50) or AMT ($187.50/2,160 min) annotation, a relevant cost comparator if c22's diagnostic per-checker verdicts were ever to be judge-based rather than purely deterministic-code-based.
quotes: "This paper introduces the Decomposed Requirements Following Ratio (DRFR), a new metric for evaluating Large Language Models' (LLMs) ability to follow instructions." (Abstract)
"It is important to highlight that even minor deviations from the requirement result in a negative (NO) response, as precise adherence is mandatory." (Sec. 2.1)

"As for multiple decomposed questions, We engage in a multi-turn dialogue with GPT-4. Each instruction and generation is followed by several questions, asked sequentially. GPT-4's response to each question is used as context for the subsequent question. We also explored different methods, including asking all questions simultaneously and asking each question independently, but found the sequential multi-turn dialogue to yield more effective results." (Appendix A.2)
"Notably, the 'Actual Instruction' is not included in the Decomposed Protocol's prompt, as we hypothesize that the decomposed questions encompass the instruction's requirements." (Appendix A.2)

"The observed gap exceeding 0.19 between the DRFR and Direct Scoring (DS) suggests that DRFR yields a notably higher annotator consensus, thereby implying its enhanced reliability for human annotations." (Sec. 3.1)

"GPT-4 exhibits superior performance over the other LLMs, scoring the highest amongst all. However, even with this state-of-the-art model, more than 10% of the requirements remain unfulfilled, indicating a critical need for further enhancement within the aspect of instruction following." (Sec. 4)
"performance is highest on Content and Style, intermediate on Format, and lowest on Number and Linguistic constraints. This general trend is observed across all six language models." (Sec. 4)
